topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed a monotonous tune, mirroring the dull throb behind Sarah’s eyes. She watched Mark stride confidently toward the executive suite, his tie perfectly knotted, radiating an energy she simply couldn't muster. *Of course,* she thought, picking at a loose thread on her sleeve. It had been this way for months—the unspoken acknowledgment that Mark was the obvious choice, the one who always seemed to be three steps ahead. Sarah adjusted her posture, smoothing down her skirt, a small, almost imperceptible tightening in her jaw. She offered a polite, brittle smile when Mark paused by the water cooler, a gesture practiced until it felt like a costume. Inside, there was just a quiet settling, a letting go of the fight. She turned back to her keyboard, the click of the keys sounding unnervingly loud in the sudden vacuum of her attention.

[story 2]

"Congratulations, Mark. You’ve earned it," the manager had said, clapping him heartily on the shoulder. Sarah stood near the doorway, nursing a lukewarm coffee. The warmth of the praise felt distant, like a signal meant for someone else. She watched the celebratory chatter fade as Mark was ushered away, his laughter echoing down the hall. A heavy cloak seemed to settle around her shoulders, making her movements slow and deliberate. She took a slow sip of her coffee, the bitter taste grounding her in the present moment. There was no surge of anger, just a deep, flat acceptance of the current arrangement. She finally pushed off the wall, the motion weary, and walked toward the filing cabinet, her footsteps barely disturbing the carpet.

[story 3]

I stared at the framed memo on the wall, the one detailing the promotion criteria. My gaze drifted from the meticulously worded requirements to the empty space beside my own name. It was a familiar scene: Mark, buoyant and assured, being handed the keys to the next level. I felt a strange hollowness expand in my chest, a sensation akin to having run out of air. I didn't bother to look up when my supervisor approached, his expression one of professional goodwill. "Good work this quarter, Sarah. We'll be in touch soon." The words landed without impact. I nodded, a small, stiff movement, and focused intently on organizing the papers on my desk, each file folder a small, manageable weight in a world that suddenly felt too large and indifferent.
